narrative:
As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model estimated a 65% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt—a threshold that led to the rejection of your application, as schools aim to admit those with stronger predicted odds of passing. This decision hinged primarily on three key factors from your academic record that pulled the prediction toward failure.

The most influential was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3rd, which is below the typical 5.5 average; this lower performance in your later undergraduate years signaled to the model a potential struggle with the sustained rigor needed for bar success. Next, your family income bracket, while upper-middle, contributed toward the failure prediction in this context, as the model associates certain socioeconomic patterns with bar outcomes in ways that didn't favor your profile here. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, lower than the average 3.21, further reinforced this direction, suggesting the model saw it as a modest but notable drag on your predicted bar performance.

For comparison, typical admitted students often have higher decile rankings around the 5th to 6th level, stronger GPAs closer to 3.2 or above, and family backgrounds that align more favorably with bar-passing trends in the dataset. Other aspects, like your LSAT score of 33 (below the 36.7 average) and full-time student status—which is common for 93% of students—played lesser roles and didn't offset the top concerns. While these predictions aren't destiny, they reflect patterns from real student data.

To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or UGPA-boosting courses to strengthen your metrics, targeting improvements in later-year performance like your deciles. Seeking targeted bar prep resources or mentorship from law alumni could also help demonstrate growth. Reapplying after addressing these areas might shift your predicted odds in a future review.
